concepts.title: Author's Intent
concepts.description: The story 'Should Wizard Hit Mommy?' is a satire written by Randall Jarrell. The author's primary intent is to question and critique the societal norms and expectations of the 1950s, specifically focusing on the nuclear family and the roles of parents.

concepts.title: The Wizard as a Symbol
concepts.description: The wizard represents the father figure in the story, embodying the societal expectations of masculinity and authority. His actions and decisions are driven by a desire to maintain this image and prove his worth as a provider.

concepts.title: The Mother's Rebellion
concepts.description: The mother's rebellion against the wizard's authoritarian rule is a pivotal moment in the story. It highlights her growing frustration with the societal expectations placed upon her and her desire for autonomy and self-expression.

concepts.title: Identifying Satire
concepts.description: To identify satire in the story, remember the acronym 'SATIRE': S - Sarcasm, A - Absurdity, T - Tone, I - Irony, R - Ridicule, E - Exaggeration. The story employs these elements to critique societal norms and expectations.

concepts.title: Misinterpreting the Wizard's Character
concepts.description: Students often misinterpret the wizard's character as a one-dimensional figure, failing to recognize the complexities of his personality and the societal pressures that shape his actions. To avoid this, remember that the wizard is a product of his time and is struggling to maintain his image as a provider and authority figure.

concepts.title: The Six-Year-Old's Perspective
concepts.description: The six-year-old's perspective is a crucial element of the story, providing insight into the child's innocence and naivety. Remember the key points: the child sees the wizard as a symbol of authority, the mother as a source of comfort, and the nuclear family as a source of security and happiness.

concepts.title: Social Commentary
concepts.description: The story offers a scathing social commentary on the nuclear family and the roles of parents in the 1950s. It critiques the societal expectations placed upon women and the pressure to conform to traditional gender roles.

concepts.title: The Child's Innocence
concepts.description: The six-year-old's innocence and naivety serve as a foil to the adult characters' complexities. The child's perspective highlights the artificial nature of the nuclear family and the societal expectations placed upon it.
